content.problemStatement:
Chef has NNN items in his shop (numbered 111 through NNN); for each valid iii, the price of the iii-th item is PiP_iPi​. Since Chef has very loyal customers, all NNN items are guaranteed to be sold regardless of their price.

However, the government introduced a price ceiling KKK, which means that for each item iii such that Pi>KP_i \gt KPi​>K, its price should be reduced from PiP_iPi​ to KKK.

Chef's revenue is the sum of prices of all the items he sells. Find the amount of revenue which Chef loses because of this price ceiling.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers N and K. The second line contains N space‑separated integers P1,P2,…,PN representing the original prices of the items.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the total revenue lost because of the price ceiling.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Prices above 4 are 10 and 5, loss = (10-4)+(5-4)=7.\nTest case 2: No price exceeds 15, loss = 0.\nTest case 3: All prices exceed 5, loss = (10-5)+(9-5)+(8-5)+(7-5)+(6-5)=15.
All prices are equal to the ceiling, so no revenue is lost.

hidden.label: edge case – ceiling higher than all prices
general case 2 – some equal to ceiling

solution.approach: For each test case iterate over the N prices. If a price Pi is greater than K, the revenue loss contributed by that item is Pi‑K. Accumulate this value in a 64‑bit integer (long long / long) because the sum can be up to N·1000. After processing all items output the accumulated loss. The algorithm is optimal because each price must be examined at least once.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.
